title: Prototype Chain Lookup — hasOwnProperty
output:
Rex makes a sound
true
false
explanation: speak is on Array.prototype, not on dog. hasOwnProperty('name') is true (set on instance via this.name). hasOwnProperty('speak') is false (it's inherited).
mentalModel: Own properties live on the instance. Prototype properties are inherited via the chain

title: Object.create — Prototype Without Constructor
explanation: Object.create(parent) sets parent as child's prototype. greet is inherited (hasOwnProperty = false). name is an own property (hasOwnProperty = true).
mentalModel: Object.create() sets the prototype explicitly. The child inherits but doesn't own parent methods

title: Static Method — Not on Prototype
output: TypeError: member.getFullName is not a function
explanation: getFullName was added to Person (the constructor function), NOT to Person.prototype. Instances don't have access to static methods — only to prototype methods.
mentalModel: Static methods (on constructor) ≠ prototype methods (on instances). Use .prototype for inheritance

title: delete on Prototype — Affects All Instances
output: TypeError: pet.bark is not a function
explanation: delete removes bark from the prototype. Since ALL instances share Dog.prototype, bark disappears for every Dog instance. pet.bark is undefined → TypeError.
mentalModel: Deleting from the prototype affects ALL instances — the property disappears from the chain

title: Extending Built-in Prototypes
explanation: Adding a method to String.prototype makes it available on ALL strings. Primitives auto-box to their wrapper objects to access methods. This works but is considered bad practice.
mentalModel: You can extend built-in prototypes (but shouldn't). Primitives auto-box to access methods

title: Class Inheritance — super() Order Matters
explanation: In derived classes, super() must be called BEFORE accessing 'this'. super(name) calls Dog's constructor, setting this.name. Then this.size is set. Both properties are on the instance.
mentalModel: In a subclass, must call super() before 'this'. super() runs the parent constructor

title: Arrow Functions Have No prototype Property
explanation: Regular functions have a .prototype property (an object with a constructor). Arrow functions do NOT have a .prototype — they can't be used as constructors with 'new'.
mentalModel: Arrow functions have no .prototype, no 'this', no 'arguments', and can't be called with 'new'

title: Class constructor — extends Flamingo/Bird
explanation: Flamingo's constructor runs first, logging 'I'm pink.' Then super() calls Bird's constructor, logging 'I'm a bird.' Console output follows the call order.
mentalModel: Code before super() runs before the parent constructor. super() is the handoff point

title: typeof and instanceof — Classes are Functions
explanation: Classes are syntactic sugar for constructor functions. Instances created with 'new' are objects. typeof any instance = 'object'. Note: typeof Person = 'function'.
mentalModel: Classes = sugar over constructor functions. Instances are always 'object', classes are 'function'

title: Class Reassignment — Constructor Name
explanation: Classes are objects (references). AnotherPerson = Person copies the reference — both variables point to the same class. new AnotherPerson('Sarah') is identical to new Person('Sarah').
mentalModel: Classes are first-class values. Assigning a class to another variable copies the reference
